hints: {a1} Formula: **would/could/shall/can + base verb**. Usage: used for polite requests and offers. Markers: please, mind, polite request. Example: *Mike, would you mind not doing that?*.

a1.can: ❌ «can» is a less polite form. Use would/could. Example: *Mike, would you mind not doing that?*.
a1.will: ❌ «will» sounds like an order, not a request. Example: *Mike, would you mind not doing that?*.
a1.would: ✅ «would» is a polite form for a request. Example: *Mike, would you mind not doing that?*.
a1.shall: ❌ The option is not suitable for a polite request. Example: *Mike, would you mind not doing that?*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **can + base verb**. Usage: expresses ability or skill. Markers: speak, ability, skill. Example: *I can speak six languages.*.

a1.may: ❌ «may» expresses permission or possibility, not ability. Example: *I can speak six languages.*.
a1.can: ✅ «can» expresses ability or skill. Example: *I can speak six languages.*.
a1.must: ❌ «must» indicates obligation, not ability. Example: *I can speak six languages.*.
a1.should: ❌ The option does not express ability. Example: *I can speak six languages.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **might/may/could + base verb**. Usage: expresses possibility or assumption. Markers: maybe, perhaps, possibly. Example: *Why hasn't she arrived yet? — She might be ill.*.

a1.must: ❌ «must» expresses certainty, not assumption. Example: *Why hasn't she arrived yet? — She might be ill.*.
a1.might: ✅ «might» indicates possibility or assumption. Example: *Why hasn't she arrived yet? — She might be ill.*.
a1.can't: ❌ «can't» means impossibility, not assumption. Example: *Why hasn't she arrived yet? — She might be ill.*.
a1.should: ❌ The option is not suitable for expressing possibility. Example: *Why hasn't she arrived yet? — She might be ill.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **would/could/shall/can + base verb**. Usage: we use it for polite requests and offers. Markers: please, mind, polite request. Example: *Could you please help me with these bags?*.

a1.could: ✅ "could" — polite form for a request. Example: *Could you please help me with these bags?*.
a1.can: ❌ "can" — less polite form. Use would/could. Example: *Could you please help me with these bags?*.
a1.shall: ❌ The option is not suitable for a polite request. Example: *Could you please help me with these bags?*.
a1.will: ❌ "will" sounds like a command, not a request. Example: *Could you please help me with these bags?*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **should (have) + base verb / V3**. Usage: we give advice or criticize a past action. Markers: advice, should have, criticism. Example: *You should have told him the truth.*.

a1.should: ✅ "should" expresses advice or criticism. Example: *You should have told him the truth.*.
a1.must: ❌ The option does not convey advice properly. Example: *You should have told him the truth.*.
a1.might: ❌ The option does not convey advice properly. Example: *You should have told him the truth.*.
a1.can: ❌ The option does not convey advice properly. Example: *You should have told him the truth.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **mustn't / can't + base verb**. Usage: we express a strict prohibition. Markers: forbidden, prohibited, not allowed. Example: *You mustn't bring animals into England.*.

a1.can't: ❌ "can't" means impossibility, not prohibition. Example: *You mustn't bring animals into England.*.
a1.mustn't: ✅ "mustn't" expresses a strict prohibition. Example: *You mustn't bring animals into England.*.
a1.shouldn't: ❌ "shouldn't" — this is advice, not a strict prohibition. Example: *You mustn't bring animals into England.*.
a1.may not: ❌ The option does not express a strict prohibition. Example: *You mustn't bring animals into England.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **won't + base verb**. Usage: we express refusal or unwillingness. Markers: refusal, unwilling, resistance. Example: *The piano is so heavy. It won't move.*.

a1.can't: ❌ The option does not show refusal or unwillingness. Example: *The piano is so heavy. It won't move.*.
a1.won't: ✅ "won't" shows refusal or unwillingness. Example: *The piano is so heavy. It won't move.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ The option does not show refusal or unwillingness. Example: *The piano is so heavy. It won't move.*.
a1.shouldn't: ❌ The option does not show refusal or unwillingness. Example: *The piano is so heavy. It won't move.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **shall we + base verb**. Usage: we make a suggestion or ask for an opinion. Markers: suggestion, proposal, invite. Example: *Shall we invite the Smiths for the party?*.

a1.should: ❌ The option is not suitable for a suggestion. Example: *Shall we invite the Smiths for the party?*.
a1.will: ❌ The option is not suitable for a suggestion. Example: *Shall we invite the Smiths for the party?*.
a1.shall: ✅ "shall" makes a suggestion or asks for an opinion. Example: *Shall we invite the Smiths for the party?*.
a1.must: ❌ The option is not suitable for a suggestion. Example: *Shall we invite the Smiths for the party?*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **can't / cannot + base verb**. Usage: we express impossibility or inability. Markers: unable, impossible, cannot. Example: *I'm afraid I can't go with you.*.

a1.won't: ❌ "won't" expresses refusal, not impossibility. Example: *I'm afraid I can't go with you.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ "mustn't" means prohibition, not impossibility. Example: *I'm afraid I can't go with you.*.
a1.can't: ✅ "can't" expresses impossibility. Example: *I'm afraid I can't go with you.*.
a1.shouldn't: ❌ The option does not express impossibility. Example: *I'm afraid I can't go with you.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **should (have) + base verb / V3**. Usage: we give advice or criticize a past action. Markers: advice, should have, criticism. Example: *My parents say I shouldn't eat so many sweets.*.

a1.mustn't: ❌ The option does not convey advice properly. Example: *My parents say I shouldn't eat so many sweets.*.
a1.can't: ❌ The option does not convey advice properly. Example: *My parents say I shouldn't eat so many sweets.*.
a1.shouldn't: ✅ «shouldn't» expresses advice or criticism. Example: *My parents say I shouldn't eat so many sweets.*.
a1.won't: ❌ The option does not convey advice properly. Example: *My parents say I shouldn't eat so many sweets.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **must / have to + base verb**. Usage: we express necessity or obligation. Markers: necessary, must, have to. Example: *I have a wedding party at the weekend. I must buy a new dress and a pair of shoes.*.

a1.must: ✅ «must» indicates necessity. Example: *I have a wedding party at the weekend. I must buy a new dress and a pair of shoes.*.
a1.should: ❌ The option does not show necessity properly. Example: *I have a wedding party at the weekend. I must buy a new dress and a pair of shoes.*.
a1.might: ❌ The option does not show necessity properly. Example: *I have a wedding party at the weekend. I must buy a new dress and a pair of shoes.*.
a1.may: ❌ The option does not show necessity properly. Example: *I have a wedding party at the weekend. I must buy a new dress and a pair of shoes.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **may / might / could + base verb**. Usage: we politely ask for permission. Markers: permission, polite, formal. Example: *I was wondering if I may ask you for a favour.*.

a1.may: ✅ «may» is a polite form for asking for permission. Example: *I was wondering if I may ask you for a favour.*.
a1.might: ❌ The option is less polite for asking for permission. Example: *I was wondering if I may ask you for a favour.*.
a1.could: ❌ The option is less polite for asking for permission. Example: *I was wondering if I may ask you for a favour.*.
a1.can: ❌ The option is less polite for asking for permission. Example: *I was wondering if I may ask you for a favour.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **must + base verb**. Usage: we give strong advice or recommendation. Markers: strong advice, recommendation, must see. Example: *Ariel: Ethan, Clint Eastwood's *Hereafter* is at the Odeon this weekend. We must see it.*.

a1.must: ✅ «must» gives strong advice or recommendation. Example: *Ariel: Ethan, Clint Eastwood's *Hereafter* is at the Odeon this weekend. We must see it.*.
a1.can: ❌ The option does not convey the strength of the recommendation. Example: *Ariel: Ethan, Clint Eastwood's *Hereafter* is at the Odeon this weekend. We must see it.*.
a1.might: ❌ The option does not convey the strength of the recommendation. Example: *Ariel: Ethan, Clint Eastwood's *Hereafter* is at the Odeon this weekend. We must see it.*.
a1.should: ❌ The option does not convey the strength of the recommendation. Example: *Ariel: Ethan, Clint Eastwood's *Hereafter* is at the Odeon this weekend. We must see it.*.

hints:
{a1} Formula: **can't / cannot + base verb**. Usage: we express impossibility or inability. Markers: unable, impossible, cannot. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
{a2} Formula: **must / have to + base verb**. Usage: we express obligation or forced necessity. Markers: obligation, duty, required. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.

a1.can't: ✅ «can't» expresses impossibility. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
a1.mustn't: ❌ «mustn't» means prohibition, not impossibility. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
a1.shouldn't: ❌ The option does not express impossibility. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
a1.won't: ❌ «won't» expresses refusal, not impossibility. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.

a2.must: ✅ «must» expresses obligation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
a2.should: ❌ The option does not express obligation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
a2.may: ❌ The option does not express obligation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.
a2.will: ❌ The option does not express obligation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but I'm afraid I can't this weekend. I must go to Ipswich. It's my grandmother's birthday.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **will + base verb**. Usage: we express a spontaneous decision or intention. Markers: decision, intention, spontaneous. Example: *Ariel: Well, I will call David. I'm sure he wants to see it.*.

a1.will: ✅ «will» shows a spontaneous decision. Example: *Ariel: Well, I will call David. I'm sure he wants to see it.*.
a1.shall: ❌ The option does not show a spontaneous decision. Example: *Ariel: Well, I will call David. I'm sure he wants to see it.*.
a1.must: ❌ The option does not show a spontaneous decision. Example: *Ariel: Well, I will call David. I'm sure he wants to see it.*.
a1.can: ❌ The option does not show a spontaneous decision. Example: *Ariel: Well, I will call David. I'm sure he wants to see it.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **should + base verb**. Usage: we give advice or recommendation. Markers: recommendation, advice, better. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but you should call him right away. You know how busy he is at the weekends.*.

a1.must: ❌ The option is not suitable for a recommendation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but you should call him right away. You know how busy he is at the weekends.*.
a1.should: ✅ «should» gives advice or recommendation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but you should call him right away. You know how busy he is at the weekends.*.
a1.may: ❌ The option is not suitable for a recommendation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but you should call him right away. You know how busy he is at the weekends.*.
a1.can: ❌ The option is not suitable for a recommendation. Example: *Ethan: Yeah, but you should call him right away. You know how busy he is at the weekends.*.

hints: {a1} Formula: **can + base verb**. Usage: we express ability or skill. Markers: speak, ability, skill. Example: *Ariel: Ahh! You're right, but I can talk to him tonight at Chris's birthday party.*.

a1.can: ✅ «can» expresses ability or skill. Example: *Ariel: Ahh! You're right, but I can talk to him tonight at Chris's birthday party.*.
a1.must: ❌ «must» shows obligation, not ability. Example: *Ariel: Ahh! You're right, but I can talk to him tonight at Chris's birthday party.*.
a1.should: ❌ The option does not express ability. Example: *Ariel: Ahh! You're right, but I can talk to him tonight at Chris's birthday party.*.
a1.might: ❌ «might» expresses permission or possibility, not ability. Example: *Ariel: Ahh! You're right, but I can talk to him tonight at Chris's birthday party.*.

hints:
{a1} Formula: **will + base verb**. Usage: we make a promise. Markers: promise, assurance, will do. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
{a2} Formula: **won't + base verb, will you?**. Usage: we ask someone not to do something. Markers: request, negative, please don't. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.

a1.will: ✅ «will» expresses a promise. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
a1.might: ❌ The option does not express a promise. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
a1.can: ❌ The option does not express a promise. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
a1.must: ❌ The option does not express a promise. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.

a2.won't: ✅ «won't» asks someone not to do something. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
a2.can't: ❌ The option is not suitable for a negative request. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
a2.shouldn't: ❌ The option is not suitable for a negative request. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
a2.mustn't: ❌ The option is not suitable for a negative request. Example: *Ariel: Thanks! I will be right back. Hey, you won't eat my biscuits, will you?*.
